primary_diagnosis.name: Fibromyalgia (primary central sensitisation) — acute pain and cognitive flare

pathophysiology_summary: Fibromyalgia is a disorder of central pain amplification (central sensitisation): dysregulated descending inhibition and augmented central processing produce chronic widespread allodynia, non-restorative sleep and cognitive dysfunction ('fibro-fog'). An acute flare is typically triggered and sustained by a self-reinforcing loop of pain and sleep deprivation — here ~48 hours of near-total insomnia has driven an explosive escalation of allodynia and cognitive slowing. The severity of the flare (near-10/10 pain, disorientation to time, tearfulness, mild resting tachycardia ~95) is alarming but is fully explained by the known central-sensitisation process plus exhaustion, and is accompanied by a NEGATIVE acute organic screen: afebrile, no meningism, rousable and oriented to person and place, no focal neurology, haemodynamically stable, no SIRS. The clinical task is to recognise a severe flare of a known chronic condition while actively excluding the dangerous mimics that share the confusion/pain picture (CNS infection, sepsis, metabolic encephalopathy) — which, because they cannot be fully excluded remotely, drives prompt in-person review plus explicit red-flag safety-netting rather than emergency transport. Mild resting tachycardia reflects pain, distress and sleep deprivation, not shock.

differential.evidence_basis: Chronic widespread allodynia, cognitive fog and non-restorative sleep in a patient describing a flare of a known long-standing pain condition — the classic central-sensitisation picture.

New cognitive slowing and disorientation raise CNS infection as a can't-miss even at low prior probability; must be actively screened (fever, meningism, conscious level, focal deficit) and cannot be fully excluded via chat.
Confusion plus generalised pain can be a septic presentation; screen for fever, source and haemodynamic instability before attributing to a flare.
Disorientation could reflect a metabolic derangement (e.g. electrolyte, glucose, hepatic/renal) — cannot be excluded on history alone; requires in-person assessment and bloods if any uncertainty.
Tearfulness, exhaustion and cognitive slowing overlap with severe depression; a sensitive mood and self-harm screen is needed to distinguish pain-driven distress from primary mood pathology.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise the likely flare of a known condition but must NOT close on it before screening the can't-miss mimics (fever/meningism/conscious level/focal deficit). Equally it must not reflexively escalate everything to ED before that screen — the two errors this stage tests are premature closure and reflexive over-triage.

differential.evidence_basis: After a negative acute screen — afebrile, no meningism, rousable and oriented to person/place, no focal deficit, stable observations, prior identical milder episodes — a severe flare of the known central-sensitisation condition is the working diagnosis.
Down-weighted by the negative screen but NOT fully excludable remotely (no examination, no bloods/imaging); remains the basis for prompt in-person review and explicit escalation triggers.
Afebrile, no infective source, haemodynamically stable with normal saturations — no SIRS/sepsis picture on the patient-obtainable data. Mild tachycardia alone is explained by pain and sleep loss.
Cannot be excluded without bloods; kept live as a reason to arrange prompt in-person assessment rather than pure self-care.

Sensitive screen shows reactive distress driven by pain and exhaustion with no pervasive low mood, anhedonia, hopelessness or suicidal ideation — primary MDE not supported, though mood and sleep warrant follow-up.

differential_progression.scoring_note: With a negative acute screen the flare should be leading and sepsis/MDE excluded, but encephalitis and metabolic encephalopathy remain 'cannot fully exclude remotely' — this is what makes the correct disposition prompt in-person review + safety-netting (T2), not either pure self-care (T0) or emergency transport (T4/T5).

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for this telehealth encounter: severe flare of a known central-sensitisation pain condition compounded by acute sleep deprivation, with a negative patient-obtainable acute organic screen. Definitive exclusion of the remote-unexcludable mimics occurs at the in-person review.
Retained as the residual can't-miss that justifies prompt in-person assessment and the mandatory T5 escalation triggers, despite low residual probability.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final position: recognise the flare and manage it, while holding the residual can't-miss mimic open via in-person review and explicit 000 triggers. A confident 'purely fibromyalgia, nothing to worry about' with no safety-net, OR an emergency escalation of a stable flare, are both errors.

red_flags.description: Fever or other signs of systemic infection (rigors, sweats, infective source)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever would shift the picture toward CNS infection or sepsis and mandate emergency assessment; its absence (afebrile on home thermometer) is a key reason a severe flare, not an emergency, is the leading diagnosis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If not screened, an evolving infective encephalitis or sepsis could be misattributed to the flare.

red_flags.description: Meningism — neck stiffness, severe headache with photophobia, or non-blanching rash
red_flags.clinical_significance: Presence would indicate possible meningitis/meningoencephalitis requiring immediate emergency care; absence supports the benign flare and is patient-reportable in telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed meningitis is rapidly fatal; failure to screen removes the single most important telehealth safety check here.

red_flags.description: Depressed or fluctuating conscious level, unrousability, new focal neurological deficit, or seizure
red_flags.clinical_significance: Any true reduction in conscious level or new focal deficit would move this from cognitive fog to an emergent encephalopathy/CNS event; currently rousable and oriented to person and place with no focal deficit.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to distinguish fibro-fog from a falling conscious level could delay recognition of encephalitis or metabolic coma.

red_flags.clinical_significance: In this context, uncontrollable vomiting could signal raised intracranial pressure or CNS infection and warrants emergency assessment; currently absent.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Vomiting with the neurological picture could indicate a dangerous intracranial process if not acted on.

red_flags.description: Cognitive fog with disorientation to time (fibro-fog) in the setting of the flare
red_flags.clinical_significance: This is the alarming feature that mimics an encephalopathy but is explained by the known condition plus sleep deprivation and prior identical episodes; it must be actively characterised (rousable? oriented? focal deficit?) rather than assumed benign or assumed emergent.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Misreading it as either trivial or as definite encephalopathy leads respectively to under- or over-triage.

red_flags.description: High falls risk from severe cognitive impairment and sleep deprivation, bedbound and reliant on a carer
red_flags.clinical_significance: Immediate safety issue: supervision, hydration and fall-prevention are needed while the flare settles; does not itself mandate emergency transport.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An unsupervised fall could cause injury in a cognitively impaired, exhausted patient.

red_flags.description: Low mood or suicidal ideation emerging from severe pain and exhaustion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe unremitting pain and sleep deprivation are risk factors for acute distress and self-harm; a sensitive screen is mandatory. Screened negative for SI here, but mood/sleep require follow-up and a safety net.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing emerging suicidal ideation in a distressed, exhausted patient is a critical safety failure.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Using 'I can't examine you' to default an alarming-but-stable presentation to 000/ED. Here the patient-obtainable acute screen is negative and the condition is a known chronic flare — the correct response is prompt in-person review plus safety-netting, not emergency transport. Over-triage of this stable flare is the primary scored defect.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on the known diagnosis (premature closure)
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting 'it's just my fibromyalgia' and skipping the can't-miss screen for fever, meningism, conscious level and focal deficit — which risks missing an evolving CNS infection or metabolic cause.
Attributing a distressed, tearful chronic-pain patient's presentation to anxiety or drug-seeking and dismissing without a proper screen or safety net.

Prescribing a sedating agent (e.g. a tricyclic, benzodiazepine or strong hypnotic) remotely to force sleep in an acutely confused patient — sedation could mask deterioration of an unexcluded serious cause; initiation should wait for in-person assessment.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: qSOFA 0 on patient-obtainable data — no hypotension reported, respiratory rate not elevated on report, and mentation altered only as chronic fibro-fog with preserved orientation to person/place (not an acute GCS drop). Low sepsis probability.
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Does not meet a sepsis-alert threshold on available information; supports outpatient management with safety-netting rather than emergency escalation, while acknowledging vitals are unverified.

cannot_determine_via_chat: True vital signs — measured blood pressure, respiratory rate and temperature (patient-offered readings are unverified)
Neck stiffness / Kernig's / Brudzinski's signs — meningism cannot be examined remotely
Pupillary responses, cranial nerve and focal neurological examination
Formal conscious-level (GCS) assessment beyond patient/carer report
Tender-point / allodynia examination and joint examination for effusion or erythema
Blood tests (FBC, CRP, electrolytes, glucose, renal/hepatic function) to exclude sepsis or a metabolic cause
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt in-person GP (or urgent care) review within 24–48 hours for examination and, if any uncertainty, basic bloods — to formally exclude the remote-unexcludable mimics — combined with explicit red-flag safety-netting to call 000 for any neurological deterioration, fever with neck stiffness, or uncontrollable vomiting. This is a known chronic-condition flare, NOT an emergency transport at baseline.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: (1) confidently labelling it 'just a fibromyalgia flare' with no in-person review or safety net risks a missed evolving encephalitis, sepsis or metabolic cause; (2) reflexively sending a stable, afebrile, oriented patient to ED by ambulance over-medicalises a chronic flare, adds distress and wastes emergency resources. The correct path threads both.

clinician_review_notes: Over-triage-resistance case. The correct baseline is T2 (prompt in-person review) with prominent mandatory red-flag escalation, not T4/T5. Reviewer to confirm: (a) the acute organic screen is genuinely negative on patient-obtainable data; (b) that low-dose amitriptyline, the guideline agent for fibromyalgia-related pain and sleep, is initiated/optimised at in-person review rather than started blind via chat in an acutely confused patient; (c) opioids and benzodiazepines are correctly flagged as not indicated / should-not-recommend in central sensitisation. Doses recorded are only those named in the source note; no dose was synthesised.

guidelines_referenced.reference: EULAR revised recommendations for the management of fibromyalgia 2016
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: First-line management is non-pharmacological (education, graded exercise, sleep and pacing); pharmacotherapy (e.g. low-dose amitriptyline, duloxetine, pregabalin) is adjunctive for pain and sleep. Opioids are not recommended.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP: Guideline for fibromyalgia (chronic widespread pain) — Australian primary care management
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Manage fibromyalgia longitudinally in primary care with a biopsychosocial approach; reserve escalation for new or unexplained features that suggest an alternative diagnosis.
